critique.summary: The article announces the release of Java 26, detailing its new features, improvements, and preview updates across various Java Enhancement Proposals (JEPs). It highlights key additions such as enhanced garbage collection, HTTP/3 support, structured concurrency, lazy constants, and updates to pattern matching and vector APIs, while also discussing deprecations and preparations for future Java developments.
mainPoints: Java 26 introduces new features including ahead-of-time object caching compatible with any garbage collector (JEP 516) and improved G1 garbage collector throughput by reducing synchronization (JEP 522).
The HTTP Client API now supports HTTP/3 (JEP 517), allowing faster and more reliable web communication with fallback to older protocols if necessary.
Structured concurrency (JEP 525) is previewed to improve concurrency management by enforcing task hierarchies and better error handling.
Lazy constants (JEP 526) provide a new way to defer immutable object initialization safely and efficiently, improving startup performance.
The Vector API (JEP 529) continues incubation, enabling high-performance vector computations leveraging SIMD instructions.
Pattern matching enhancements (JEP 530) extend support to primitive types in instanceof and switch constructs, improving code expressiveness and safety.
Java 26 deprecates unsafe mutation of final fields via deep reflection, preparing for stricter integrity enforcement in future releases (JEP 500).
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the features and changes introduced in Java 26 based on official Java Enhancement Proposals (JEPs) and the OpenJDK project documentation. The details about garbage collection improvements, HTTP/3 support, structured concurrency, lazy constants, vector API, pattern matching, and deprecations align with publicly available information from the OpenJDK and Oracle sources. The explanations of technical concepts and code examples are consistent with the official JEPs and Java development practices. The article does not present unsupported claims or sensational framing and provides a comprehensive overview of the release.
considerations: The article assumes familiarity with Java development concepts and JEPs, which may limit accessibility for beginners.
It does not discuss potential backward compatibility issues or migration challenges developers might face when adopting Java 26 features.
The preview features require enabling preview flags, which may affect stability and production readiness; this caution is mentioned but could be emphasized more.
The article does not provide external perspectives or critiques, such as community feedback or performance benchmarks from independent sources.
Security implications of new features, especially around lazy constants and HTTP/3, are not deeply explored.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion reflects a mix of appreciation for Java's evolution and criticism of its culture, legacy issues, and ecosystem challenges. Several commenters highlight improvements in recent Java versions, while others express frustration with its complexity and slow adoption in areas like Android.

comments.author: ludovicianul
comments.text: I program in Java for more than 15 years now. I can resonate with people hating the language from it's early days due to the experience with all the enterprisy features and over abstractions. Or confunding Java with the Spring ecosystem. But Java came a long way over the years. It's now what many would call a "modern" language. It's less verbose, has many of the features people find appealing in Scala and Kotlin and it can even compile to native binaries using GraalVM. This made building CLIs in Java feasible. Or lambdas.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a knowledgeable and balanced view of Java's evolution, accurately noting key improvements and modern features.

comments.author: xeubie
comments.text:
I think astronomers could measure the age of the universe in nano-Valhallas. Every year, it feels 50% closer to completion...

In all seriousness I'm happy with what Mr. Goetz and the team have done. Sealed interfaces (java 17) + exhaustive switch statements (java 21) means we now have union types in java! And instead of jumping on the async/await bandwagon we now have a more general solution that doesn't lead to API duplication (virtual threads). But Valhalla has been a veeery long time coming.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly identifies recent Java features and improvements, showing understanding of language design and concurrency models.

comments.author: einrealist
comments.text: I have been using Java since version 1.4. Both the language and its ecosystem have come a long way since then. I endured the height of the EJB phase. I adopted Spring when version 1.2 was released. I spent hours fighting with IDEs to run OSGi bundles. I hated building UIs with Swing/AWT, many of which are still in use today and are gradually being replaced by lovely JavaFX. When I look at code I wrote around 12 years ago, I'm amazed at how much I've matured too.
comments.analysis: Provides a credible historical perspective on Java's ecosystem and tooling evolution, reflecting real-world experience.

comments.author: vyskocilm
comments.text:
JEP 504: Remove the Applet API

Glad to see this being removed. Java plugins especially on Linux were awful and required by tons of corporate stuff. Anyone remeber IcedTea Web? A functional and opensource Java plugin and Java Webstart implementation?
comments.analysis: Accurately notes the removal of outdated APIs and the historical pain points with Java plugins, contributing useful context.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
the people that work on Java & the JVM are very smart.

it has become a best of breed language - hell its better than Go for industry purposes.

the drawback with Java will always be the CULTURE - (maybe someone can insert a quote of how in physics progress is only made, when old physicist die - I don't wanna be morbid ) but with Java same that's when the culture will change.

All those people using typescript (could be using Java - but the culture doesn't want them and consider them heretics for not embracing religion of OOP and FactoryFactory)
comments.analysis: Insightful cultural critique though somewhat subjective; the claim about Java being better than Go is debatable but reflects a common industry perspective.

comments.author: haolez
comments.text: I was pretty surprised when I learned recently that the Java alternative for green threads doesn't use colored functions. It put Java in a higher place in my perception.
comments.analysis: Correctly notes a technical detail about Java's virtual threads, showing understanding of concurrency implementation.

comments.author: nameconflicts
comments.text: I think Java became popular because of Sun. My experience with Java-based apps is bad. Laggy, resource-heavy, IDE bound, and it causes premature hardware performance degradation.
comments.analysis: This comment is overly negative and lacks nuance; performance depends on many factors and generalizing Java as hardware degrading is inaccurate.

comments.author: larrylawnmower
comments.text: Are people still using gigantic xml file abominations (like pom.xml) with java? No thanks. Web moved years ago, and now most LOB software appear to also have switched to languages that are not that heavy weight and riddled with software patterns. And then the sdk still require accepting an EULA. Gtfo.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid criticism about legacy build tools and XML configuration in Java, reflecting a common frustration among developers.

comments.author: olivia-banks
comments.text: Really glad to see we're getting a native PEM API.
comments.analysis: Brief but accurate comment on a specific new API addition, showing awareness of security-related improvements.

comments.author: jesse_dot_id
comments.text: Cool. I feel as though I'll never be able to escape 8, or ignore Ellison's legacy, unfortunately.
comments.analysis: Expresses a common sentiment about the long-lasting influence of Java 8 and its design choices, though somewhat vague.
